title: Chapter 17: Channeling
content: Xue learned faster than anyone Ren had ever trained.

In his previous life—his previous *timeline*—the War God had trained hundreds of soldiers in dimensional awareness, array manipulation, and the direct-channeling techniques that let a human body serve as a conduit for frequencies beyond the visible spectrum. The best of them had taken months to achieve basic proficiency.
Xue achieved it in eleven days.

She trained in secret—in her mother's old bedroom, which she'd sealed with a bloodline lock after discovering the journal. The room was insulated, private, and still resonated with Meilin's frequency signature—a latent dimensional hum that the Salvation System used as a teaching aid.
Ren stood across from her now, his enhanced senses monitoring her biometrics as she attempted the third channeling exercise: creating a sustained resonance at a specific frequency.
"Focus on the pendant," he said. "It's already tuned to your bloodline. Let it guide you—don't force the frequency, *find* it."

Xue's eyes were closed. Her hand rested on the jade pendant, fingers splayed across the cool surface. Her breathing had slowed to four cycles per minute—a combat breathing pattern Ren had taught her on day three.
The pendant began to glow.
Faint at first—barely visible even to Ren's enhanced sight. Then brighter, a green-gold pulse that synchronized with Xue's heartbeat, growing stronger with each cycle.
"She's resonating," the Salvation System noted. "Frequency: 7.83 hertz, base Schumann resonance. She's locked onto the dimensional baseline."
The room hummed. The air pressure shifted. Ren's enhanced hearing caught the subsonic vibration—a pure tone that resonated through his bones, the fundamental frequency of the containment seal.
Xue opened her eyes. They were glowing—not gold like the Abyss spore, but green, the color of the jade pendant, the color of the containment array.
"I can feel it," she breathed. "The seal. It's *everywhere*. In the walls, in the ground, in the air. It's been singing my whole life and I never heard it until now."
"Don't lose the frequency. Hold it for thirty seconds."
She held it for ninety.

When she released the channel, she sagged, and Ren caught her before she hit the floor. Her skin was cold—channeling drew thermal energy from the body to power the dimensional resonance—but her pulse was strong.
"You're a natural," he said, helping her to the chair by the window.
"I'm my mother's daughter." She accepted a cup of hot tea with shaking hands. "She channeled for years. It's in my blood."
"Your mother was talented. You're exceptional. The bloodline purity is stronger in you than it was in her—the system estimates your inherent capacity at four times the minimum channeling threshold."
Xue sipped her tea, processing. "What does that mean practically?"

"It means you could, with training, channel enough power to neutralize a node. Not re-seal it entirely, but disrupt it. Force it back into containment mode." Ren sat on the edge of the desk. "It also means your father underestimates you. He's been keeping you in business management because he thinks your bloodline value is passive—a key, not a weapon. He's wrong."

"My father underestimates a lot of things." Xue's voice had an edge now—the edge of someone who'd spent twelve years being underestimated and was no longer interested in modesty. "When this is over, he's going to regret that."
*When this is over.* She was thinking about the future now—about surviving, about winning. Not about whether to fight.
"Tomorrow we try frequency location," Ren said. "If you can produce your mother's signature frequency, the coordinates in the journal's cipher might lead us to Node 4."
"One more node out of his reach." Xue's hand found the pendant. "How many does he need to activate the portal?"
"All seven. He currently has four in his control, with the Bronze Bell partially compromised. If we secure Node 4, he'll need three more—including the pendant around your neck—to complete the array."
"So the pendant is the most important piece on the board."
"It's the piece he *can't* take," Ren said. "Because taking it means taking you. And you're the only bloodline channeler in the family who's still fully human and not under his control."
"Feng—"

"Feng is compromised. You know that now. The Patriotch has three full bloodline channelers available: himself, Feng, and you. But Feng's infection means his channeling is unstable—contaminated by Abyss frequencies. And your father won't channel himself during the final activation because the blood sacrifice required would—"
He stopped. The words had come too easily, the clinical description of a horror he'd witnessed in another timeline.
"Would what?" Xue's voice was flat.

"The final activation requires a bloodline sacrifice. The channeler who opens the portal doesn't survive. In the previous timeline, it was Feng—but the Abyss had already consumed him by then, so it didn't matter. He was a vessel, empty of everything that made him *him*." Ren forced himself to meet her eyes. "Your father is grooming Feng for that role. He's accelerating the spore infection so that by the time the final activation is ready, Feng won't be Feng anymore. He'll be a shell. A key. And your father will turn that key and not feel a thing because the son he's sacrificing will already be gone."

The room was silent. Outside, the afternoon light filtered through curtains that had hung in this room since Xue was a child, in a house that had been building toward apocalypse since before she was born.
"My father is going to use my brother as fuel," Xue said. Not a question.
"Yes."
"And when Feng is gone, who's next? What happens when the portal opens and the Abyss needs more?"

"Everyone. Everyone is next." Ren let the truth stand, ugly and unvarnished. "The portal isn't a one-time event. It's a door. Once it's open, it stays open. The Abyss consumes dimensional layers like a fire consumes oxygen. Earth is the fuel. Humanity is the fuel. Your father thinks he's special—that the entity will spare him, reward him, elevate him. But he's just another log on the fire."
Xue set down her tea. Her hands were steady.
"Then we don't just stop the portal," she said. "We destroy the mechanism. Permanently. So no one can ever open it again."

"That would require converting all seven nodes back to containment mode and then *locking* the containment. The original practitioners designed the seal to be maintained, not destroyed, but a fully locked containment array would be functionally irreversible."
"How long?"
"At my current power growth rate? A year. Maybe less, if the feedback loop continues accelerating."

"Then we have a year to seal seven nodes, save my brother, and stop my father." Xue stood up, and the morning light caught the jade pendant at her throat—green, ancient, alive with the frequency of the containment seal that had protected humanity for three thousand years. "I've handled bigger crises in Q4."
It was such a *corporate* way to frame the apocalypse that Ren almost laughed.
Almost.
One thousand seventy-five days.
